Title: NIT24 and NIT29-mediated IAA synthesis of Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzicola suppresses immunity and boosts growth in rice

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: bacterial extracellular culture / secreted by Xoc; effect assessed in rice leaves and seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: suppresses rice immune responses and promotes rice growth; enhances bacterial virulence/colonization
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step supported genetically, but rate-limiting status not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene deletion mutant; complementation; HPLC quantification of IAA; in vitro enzyme assay with purified MBP-NIT24 using IAN substrate; metabolite rescue by exogenous IAA; pathogenicity assay; bacterial population assay; ROS/callose and defense gene assays in rice; rice growth assay with fermentation broth
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: exogenous tryptophan supplementation; exogenous IAA co-treatment; rice infection conditions in greenhouse
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: comparative genomic/BLAST-based genome analysis of RS105; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: promotes IAA accumulation in Xoc, increases EPS production, enhances virulence and colonization on rice, suppresses rice ROS/callose/defense responses, and contributes to Xoc-mediated rice growth promotion
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AKO15524.1 (NIT24) was identified as a nitrilase-family IAA biosynthesis candidate in Xoc RS105. 2. Deletion of NIT24 reduced IAA content by 38.1% and strongly reduced tryptophan-stimulated IAA production. 3. Purified NIT24 showed nitrilase activity with IAN as substrate and produced IAA in vitro. 4. NIT24 loss reduced EPS production and pathogenicity, while complementation or exogenous IAA partially/completely restored these phenotypes. 5. Loss of NIT24 increased rice ROS burst, callose deposition, and defense gene expression, indicating bacterially produced IAA suppresses host immunity. 6. NIT24 contributes to Xoc-mediated promotion of rice shoot and root growth.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mutant phenotypes were only partially rescued by exogenous IAA for some traits such as EPS; homologs also occur in plants including rice; source gene is bacterial while metabolic/immune outcomes were measured in rice
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion lowers bacterial virulence and growth-promotion ability; no direct host yield tradeoff quantified
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: targeting NIT24 by antimicrobial intervention or editing/pathogen control strategies could reduce pathogen-derived IAA, thereby lowering virulence and immune suppression in rice

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: bacterial extracellular culture / secreted by Xoc; effect assessed in rice leaves and seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: suppresses rice immune responses and promotes rice growth; enhances bacterial virulence/colonization
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step supported genetically, but rate-limiting status not established
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: gene deletion mutant; complementation; HPLC quantification of IAA; in vitro enzyme assay with purified MBP-NIT29 using IAN substrate; metabolite rescue by exogenous IAA; pathogenicity assay; bacterial population assay; ROS/callose and defense gene assays in rice; rice growth assay with fermentation broth
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: exogenous tryptophan supplementation; exogenous IAA co-treatment; rice infection conditions in greenhouse
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: comparative genomic/BLAST-based genome analysis of RS105; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: promotes IAA accumulation in Xoc, increases EPS production, enhances virulence and colonization on rice, suppresses rice ROS/callose/defense responses, and contributes to Xoc-mediated rice growth promotion
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AKO15829.1 (NIT29) was identified as a nitrilase-family IAA biosynthesis candidate in Xoc RS105. 2. Deletion of NIT29 reduced IAA content by 49.1% and strongly reduced tryptophan-stimulated IAA production. 3. Purified NIT29 showed nitrilase activity with IAN as substrate and produced IAA in vitro, with higher in vitro IAA output than NIT24 under the assay shown. 4. NIT29 loss reduced EPS production and markedly reduced pathogenicity, while complementation or exogenous IAA restored these phenotypes at least partially. 5. Loss of NIT29 increased rice ROS burst, callose deposition, and defense gene expression, indicating bacterially produced IAA suppresses host immunity. 6. NIT29 contributes to Xoc-mediated promotion of rice shoot and root growth.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: RS105_ΔNIT29 often showed stronger defects than RS105_ΔNIT24, including larger reductions in IAA, EPS, and lesion length; homologs also occur in plants including rice
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion lowers bacterial virulence and growth-promotion ability; no direct host yield tradeoff quantified
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: targeting NIT29 by antimicrobial intervention or pathogen-focused engineering could reduce pathogen-derived IAA and associated immune suppression in rice
